title: Readout failures in superconducting qubits due to TLS-defects in tunnel junctions
authors: J. Lisenfeld, A. K. Händel, A. Bilmes, A. V. Ustinov
abstract: Material defects give rise to parasitic two-level systems (TLS) which present a major source of decoherence in superconducting qubits. Here, we study a strongly coupled TLS that resides in the tunnel barrier of transmon qubit. We use multi-photon spectroscopy and TLS strain tuning to explore the rich spectrum of the interacting three-partite system consisting of TLS, qubit, and its readout resonator. This reveals a strong effective resonant coupling between the TLS and the qubit's readout resonator which dresses the resonator states and results in a resonance frequency shift that spoils the readout signal. Our finding presents yet another way how material defects can interfere with qubit operation and hinder the realization of solid-state quantum processors.
summary: This paper identifies how material defects (TLS) in superconducting qubit junctions can cause readout failure by shifting the resonator frequency. By using mechanical strain to tune the defects, the authors demonstrate a strong effective coupling between the TLS and the readout resonator. This finding highlights a critical mechanism for error in solid-state quantum processors.
main_problem: The paper investigates a new source of readout failure in superconducting qubits caused by strongly coupled two-level system (TLS) defects located in the Josephson junction tunnel barriers.
main_result: The researchers identified that a TLS-resonator interaction, mediated via virtual qubit excitations, causes a resonance frequency shift in the readout resonator that is large enough to spoil the dispersive readout signal.
method: The study combines experimental strain-dependent spectroscopy using a piezo stack with numerical simulations of the system's time evolution using the QuTiP package and the Lindblad master equation.
model_or_system: The system consists of a tunable transmon qubit (Al on Si) capacitively coupled to a readout resonator, interacting with a TLS in the junction tunnel barrier. The model includes a three-partite Hamiltonian involving the qubit (Duffing oscillator), resonator (harmonic oscillator), and TLS (two-level system).
key_observables: Transmitted signal amplitude (S21), qubit energy relaxation (T1), avoided-level crossings in the resonator spectrum, and multi-photon spectroscopy transitions.
important_parameters: TLS strain-coupling strength (gamma), qubit-resonator coupling (g_qr), TLS dipole moment, and the effective TLS-resonator coupling (g_eff).
main_assumptions: The TLS-resonator interaction is assumed to be mediated via virtual qubit excitations (cross-resonance mechanism); the simulation assumes a restricted Hilbert space of 6 qubit, 6 resonator, and 2 TLS states.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the qubit circuit schematic, energy level diagrams, and spectroscopy showing avoided crossings and readout failure. Fig 2 shows multi-photon transitions at high drive power. Fig 9 displays raw S21 data vs piezo voltage and comparison with QuTiP simulations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of TLS-induced decoherence, describes the experimental setup and TLS tuning via strain, presents spectroscopic evidence of TLS-qubit-resonator interactions, details the identification of readout failure mechanisms, and concludes with a comparison of experimental data to numerical simulations.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics as it demonstrates a complex three-body interaction (TLS-qubit-resonator) and explores how environmental defects can induce effective long-range couplings and frequency shifts in a driven-dissipative system.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The color scale encodes the transmitted signal amplitude |S21| at the readout resonator frequency. Figure 1c shows avoided level crossings in both the qubit’s single-photon |0⟩→|1⟩-transition (here at 6.2 GHz), and two-photon |0⟩→|2⟩transition (here at 6.08 GHz). Such data are fitted with the help of the QuTiP software package25,26 to obtain the TLS’ parameters (see Table I) and to identify the number of involved photons in the transition.

FIG. 2. a Spectroscopy of the qubit-resonator-TLS system at enhanced drive power to observe multi-photon transitions involving higher excited states. The right inset shows the cross-section at the white dashed line. Due to the high drive power, the qubit’s |0⟩→|1⟩resonant peak at 7.3 GHz is significantly power-broadened. The tilted transitions involve TLS states whose extrapolations intersect at the resonator frequency, illustrating that the readout failure (red arrow) occurs when the TLS is resonant with the resonator at 6.63 GHz. The left inset shows a direct measurement of the resonator anti-crossing without an applied drive tone. b Transitions between eigenstates of the non-driven...

FIG. 3. a Anti-crossings between the readout resonator and the strain-tuned TLS, measured when the qubit was tuned to the indicated resonance frequencies. The effective resonator-TLS coupling strength geff is estimated from a fit (green line) and corresponds to half the distance between the two peaks at resonance as shown in the inset. b TLS-resonator coupling strength geff vs. the detuning δ = fq −fres between qubit and resonator. The solid line shows the expectation from the cross-resonance interaction, geff = gxgr/δ, for independently measured gr and gx.
